sentences.text: Yes.
The boxcar-- European boxcars are smaller than American boxcars.
I don't know in cubic feet.
I'm not too technical about describing the capacity, but I know they're smaller.
They would be perhaps like a trailer.
Like the size of an average trailer, you know, a Regal in the United States.
They're much smaller.
A boxcar would hold-- squeezed in, perhaps, something like 60, maybe 70 people.
Maybe even as much as 100, but I remember standing up.
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] This is the first boxcar I was into.
There were no guards actually inside.
The guards were outside, riding in a back car, and some on the roof.
But it was just standing-room only.
And then you were pretty well squeezed, you know?
As far as stops, we made no stops.
There were no stops for water or relieving yourself.
Whoever needed to relieve did it where he was standing.
And if someone would faint or fall down, or sleep, you would just sit on top of them.
It was just like herring.
You know, just squeeze a bunch of people in.
Similar to in a gate.
When a gate opens to a football game or something, or department store.
People-- the doors open, and people want to squeeze in through that narrow-- you know, when you see this mass of humanity squeezed, that's about-- that's what the boxcar looked like.
Pressed.
Pressed together.
Luckily, the trip was only overnight, because we arrived at the destination in a camp called Karvina.
It was on the Czech side of the border.
It was in Czechoslovakia itself.
And it was daylight already.
And the doors opened up.
And the shouts of [GERMAN].
And you jump down.
And get in formation in fives and start counting and forwards march.
And we start marching.
We marched another hour or so, came to a camp.
And it was my first sight of a camp with a gate and towers and barbed wire.
And everything was [GERMAN] and you can't miss a step.
Army.
Everything proper, in step and in formation.
As we came to the camp, there must have been quite a few bags.
I have no idea how many.
Maybe 15, 20 bags.
And a big-- each camp was-- it was like-- they say-- it was a big place.
What they call appellplatz, which was like an assembly place.
That's the place where in the morning when the bell sounds or whistle blows, you have to run out, stand in a certain place designated where your particular barrack assembles, and stand at attention.
And in fives, again, to be counted.
This is before computers.
So they counted you manually.
This was called the appellplatz.
So it was the appellplatz.
And certain barracks, you know.
And they took-- other inmates who were already there, you know, came out.
And they were sort of marching out the gate to work, you know.
And I thought, gee, this is going to be some place.
I picture myself one of them.
So they started counting us again.
Makes us stand in formation of five.
I'm standing in front, my father behind me, two or three other guys.
So fives and fives.
And a German-- the uniform in those days, this one was an SA uniform, a brown shirt.
He was the camp commandant, because that was a forced labor.
It was before concentration.
We could still keep our own clothes, except we had a star of David.
A [GERMAN], meaning Jew in German, written all across the back with paint.
And sometimes cut out pieces of-- and sewing a piece of a yellow star of David, so that you couldn't escape or have torn clothes, painted on your back.
But at least the clothes were much better than concentration camp clothes, which were a lot later.
Because this was at least regular cotton or wool.
It was warmer.
So this guy addresses us from his podium.
Again, a really tall figure, boots, and brown shirt.
And swastika and his cap, typical square cap.
And shouts, you know, the usual [GERMAN].
Nothing will happen to you.
You're here to be productive.
And if you obey orders, and do your work right, you will live, you will exist.
And for those of you who ever entertained the idea of escaping, we have some other surprises for you.
So he gave a sort of an orientation speech.
Nothing in welcoming tones, mind you.
And as he finishes, then the guy takes over after him who was a Jewish prisoner who was called the lageralteste.
Actually, the lageralteste-- to explain the prison hierarchy a little bit-- it was-- a lageralteste was really the commandant, because he had the say so in the whole camp.
He accounted to the Germans.
The Germans wouldn't be bothered with details.
He just wanted to make sure that everything functions properly, and the work is being done.
No one escapes, and don't bother me.
But the lageralteste, he was-- in English terms, would be a trustee.
And he was, of course, in charge of the whole camp.
He had under him [GERMAN], which was one who was in charge of the block, which is a barrack.
And then each barrack would be divided into six stuben, meaning rooms.
Then we had stubenalteste, meaning one in charge of the particular room.
The room had 15, 20 inmates.
He accounted to the-- each of those rooms would account to the guy who was in charge of the barrack.
And the barrack would account to the lageralteste.
The lageralteste would go to the German who was the main commandant.
As you can see, Germans know how to structure authorities.
And he gets off the podium and shouts to this lageralteste, Mailor, you take over.
His name was Mailor.
I'll never forget his name-- a rather infamous name.
And this guy takes over, and also in good German, gives us a speech.
Life here is going to be bearable if you behave, if you obey the orders.
No contraband, no this.
Anyway, then after they're both finished, they're stepping down, and they're looking at us.
Walking sort of like overseeing the troops, you know, the new arrivals.
So the German commandant in the brown uniform was first.
And I can still see him with his riding crop kind of hitting his boot as he was marching and-- or strolling and looking at row by row like this.
And all of  a sudden, my dad taps me on the shoulder.
And he says, let me change places.
Let me change places with you.
And I said, shh.
Dad, what do you want to do?
He says, please.
So I'm stepping back.
He steps up front.
And about 10, 12 feet away, the commandant slowly approaches us.
As he comes to us, my father stands among everybody, stands at attention.
And the commandant walks by.
All of a sudden, makes a double take.
He steps back and looks at my father.
He says, Abraham, [GERMAN]?
Is this you, Abraham?
And my father says, [GERMAN].
And he says, [GERMAN].
Step out.
And he turns to the-- to this Jewish guy, to the lageralteste.
And he says, Mailor, this is Abraham.
He's a coal miner.
We worked together in the coal mines.
I want this-- I want him have special treatment here.
No one lays a hand on him.
He gets special-- he gets double ration.
Anyone lays a hand on him will have me to answer-- will have to come to-- to me to answer for it.
We'll have-- I'm thinking in German.
I got mixed up my English now.
And he says-- and of course, the lageralteste, Mr.
Herr Mailor, says, [GERMAN].
And he marches on.
And my dad-- and he says, and then it was, at ease.
He's, you know, we're going to our barracks.
I said, Dad, what is he saying?
He says, yeah, I know him.
I said, you know him?
He says, of course.
He was the captain of the fire brigade at the mines, he says.
He called him by a name.
I can't think of his name.
He says, are you a friend?
He said not a close friend, but I know him.
One time we worked together, but then he became a captain.
He got an easy job.
And he says, he's not a bad guy.
And sure enough, he came-- lined up for bread, you know, my dad gets a ration.
Oh, I forgot to say, my dad says, my son is out.
My son is [GERMAN].
He said, who?
And he's pointing at me.
He says, [GERMAN].
He says, him too.
So both my dad and I.
I thought I was liberated.
I thought a miracle happened.
I mean, here in the camp.
First, the commandant-- the main guy, the god himself, knows my dad.
And true to his word, my dad got a job like a janitor.
You know, cleaning up the yard.
Didn't go out-- over there, we worked in a factory in the cold, or laying pipelines, whatever.
But not-- you know, my dad and I worked inside for about the first four to six weeks.
Then, slowly, he got reassigned to another job, another job, until they really gave him a hard job.
And then the kapo beat up on him.
And I said, Dad, you don't have to take it.
Look at it, you only got one portion.
How can you live?
I'm a little guy.
And he was a muscular man, a coal miner.
He needed more food.
And you know, I could see him going down on his weight already.
He was losing weight.
I said, go to the commandant.
He says, Henry, the commandant will stand up for me.
He says, but he said so on the appellplatz.
He said so.
I said, Dad, he said so himself.
He said, oh, that's what he said.
He says, he lives in his quarters a mile away.
This guy here is running the camp.
You know this-- the Jewish guy, Mailor.
If I go snitch on him, he says, they'll kill me.
So he didn't go to the commandant.
He was right.
And slowly, because there was some kind of an envy that this guy shouldn't have, you know, be a buddy of the commandant.
And my dad got not only a regular job, but even heavier work.
They said, well, you're a coal miner.
You should know how to load this.
Here.
Here's a shovel.
Start loading.
And I'm sorry to say that the German was not as mean as the Jewish guy.
And one other incident happened when-- at camp too-- when a guy escaped from our detail.

In Germany, the Germans have a saying-- the saying was a rule of their own, [GERMAN], which means, one for all, all for one, meaning you are responsible for-- one is responsible for all, and all are responsible for one.
If one escapes, the rest of them are punished.
We had a detail of maybe 40, 50 guys, say, laying a pipeline, digging.
One guy escaped.
They took us all into a barrack, and we got 20 lashes on our behind, because one guy escaped.
We should have warned him.
You must have seen it, and so you're responsible for him.
And the commandant-- the German commandant-- supervised the beating, except my dad again went in front of me.
When time came, he dropped his pants.
And as he lay down, the guy went over.
The commandant took the kapo who was meting out, you know, he says, pushed him away, he said, Abraham, come up.
Get out of here.
My father says, my son.
He said, get out of here.
So we stepped over to the people who were bleeding already.
So he didn't beat us.
The camp was called Karvina.
K-A-R-V-I-N-A.
June of '40-- it was about 22nd of June, 1943.
The food was distributed three times a day.
That doesn't mean we had three meals a day.
It was just distributed three times a day.
The morning was usually-- excuse me.
We're not talking about dining or sitting down.
Everything was lined up inside a barrack.
In the hallway, in front of the barrack door, there was a kettle the size of, my guess would be perhaps a 10- or 12-gallon kettle.
In the morning, it was hot black coffee, whatever.
Something that looked like coffee.
It was like some kind of an ersatz.
And you got a slice of bread.
Not a real heavy slice.
A medium slice of bread.
And sometimes on weekends, you would get a spoonful of marmalade or Jell-O or a little square of margarine spread.
So it was in the morning.
At noon, it was, again, a soup.
Maybe if you're lucky, you found a potato or two swimming in it.
And that soup-- again, this was not a dining-room arrangement.
You carried your little dish with you.
It was like an aluminum bowl-- a bowl.
And you carried them on your belt.
This was-- that and a spoon.
It was neatly shined.
You carried that on your belt.
And this was your equipment.
Without it, you were never moved anyplace.
And so you, again, got a slice of bread.
And depending how big a bread, how many people, sometimes the bread-- the rations were smaller, sometimes bigger.
Sometimes they'll slice a bread-- let's say a pound-and-a-half of bread maybe at noon maybe in 10 or 12 slices.
In the evening, it would be maybe eight slices, so the slices were thicker.
But that was your ration.
In calories, it was estimated-- I didn't know then, but as I found out reading after the war, it was designed somewhere between 600 and 700 calories a day, which is not a whole lot to exist on.
And in the evening, again, a slice of bread.
Sometimes they would have like some kind of a slice of salami.
But usually, it was a spoonful of marmalade or, as I said, Jell-O.
And some bitter, dark liquid that looks like-- looked like coffee, but it didn't taste or smell like.
That was it.
Or sometimes they would give us soup.
If there was no bread, they would give you three or four potatoes.
Both potatoes in the skin, because they wouldn't peel potatoes.
The potato would lose-- it would lose too much, so they would boil it in the skin.
And then you would pull off the skin-- depending.
If you were really hungry, eat it with the skin.
And so that was three 'meals,' quote-unquote, of the day, meals.
You focus, actually, all the time not so much on escaping, as much as on how to pull through today.
How am I going to pull through today?
Will this hold me?
Gosh, if I can just get an extra piece of bread, or extra two potato somewhere, you know?
And this camp-- this first camp at Karvina, when I still was with my dad, we were getting double rations.
That meant a lot.
Then I was cut off.
Of course, it was much worse.
Then I had this break about I still had my harmonica with me.
So word got around that I play harmonica and I would play in my room.
So I got invited to the upper hierarchy of the camp.
Not the SS, but the Jewish guy-- the commandant, the Jewish lageralteste.
And they had plenty of bread and plenty of soups.
Because first they would skim everything off the top, of course, the best.
And they and the kapos would meet and eat in a separate dining room.
So they would invite me to play something for them.
So I would play.
And then everyone would say, here, kid, have a soup.
Take this back to the barrack with you.
So the soup was kind of cold, but it settled.
It was like a Jell-O almost.
But there were potatoes in it, and some like barley maybe.
It was a really thick soup.
I never saw that kind soup in the barracks where they were, you know, where they were distributing in these little bowls.
But they really had thick soup and different soup.
So I remember the greatest thrill for me was to come, and it was in the darkness, and it was already lights out, because lights were only on for half-an-hour or an hour after work.
There was no reading period.
Just to wash up and go sleep.
And I would wake up, and I'd say, Dad, I got a soup.
And we would sit up in the bed, and we would scrape up the soup and eat the soup.
Oh, I was so happy I could share a soup with him.
That would have made my day.
As if, you know, this would really help him or help me.
But it was a morale booster.
So that camp-- that lasted till probably about November or December.
And then they liquidated that camp.
And again, we assembled and segregated.
And we were marched to another camp.
And it's a camp-- we marched to a station, rather, and again into boxcars.
And again, a day or so-- the best I remember, probably a day, maybe a day and a half, we landed in another camp close to Breslau called Dyhernfurth.
And that was spelled D-U-- umlaut-- H-R-E-M-F-U-- umlaut-- R-T-H.
Dyhernfurth.
It was somewhere close to Breslau, to Vrotslav.
And that camp was really tough.
It was without the commandant that my father knew.
It was different people and different nationalities too.
There were Ukrainians, Russians, some Czechs.
And again, we were put to work in a factory.
My father, by that time, was almost skin and bones.
How did you get to Camp Dyhernfurth from Karvina?
We again, we were marched to the railroad station and counted.
Put in boxcars.
Destination unknown, of course.
So many to a boxcar, shut the door, closed.
Except this time, there was an SS man.
I'm pretty sure, yeah.
And he kind of had a blanket.
He took space in the center.
And on the blanket and some stuff where he could lay down.
And put his gun down.
And he was in charge, kind of watching out for us.
And it was pretty cramped, but not as cramped as the first one that we went from the assembly place.
And after about a night's ride or so, we arrived the following day into this Dyhernfurth.
And again, assigned to barracks and the same detail again, being counted.
And the next day, report to so-and-so detail and go to work.
And mixing of concrete or carrying bricks, and we're building something.
I had no idea what we were building.
As it turned out, we were building more barracks, which I later found out living in, in those barracks.
So that was my second camp.
This camp?
Very sad memories, because this was the camp where I finally got separated from my father.
That was where-- they liquidated that camp.
They sent me to another camp, but they didn't take everyone.
They took the younger people and able-bodied.
And my dad was already down on his weight.
I remember a man of-- he was of medium height, maybe 5'5', 5'6', perhaps in kilos maybe 130, 140 kilos.
In pounds we're talking about-- I'm sorry, 60 or 70 kilos.
Maybe 140, 150 pounds.
And in this second camp already he was down to perhaps less than 100 pounds, maybe 90, 95 pounds.
He was skin and bones.
When took showers-- when we were supposed to take showers in the evening.
And went to showers, I could see ribs, you know, count his ribs.
And his legs were thin.
And his cheeks falling in.
Then I found out they're liquidating the camp.
And I found out my father one evening told me-- we were working separate details.
He came into my barrack.
He says, you know, I found my name on a list.
They're going to ship me to a different camp.
And I said, oh, no.
And he says well, you're in now so-and-so barrack.
And so I was really scared then.
I knew we were going to get separated.
Then the kapo told me, he says, you don't want to be barracked with your old man.
He said, he's going to pull you down.
He says, he's about had it.
They're going-- didn't you see where they're going?
They're going out to meet their maker.
You know, to him it was like talking about making reservations to go to Vegas or something.
He says, you know where they're going.
And I said, my dad is among them.
He says, kid, you try to survive yourself.
He's going to pull you down.
It's all he talking to me.
And I said to myself, how hateful.
How hateful can you be?
So I didn't listen to him.
I said, listen, maybe it's not true.
So I wanted to go into that barrack.
Then I heard rumors they're going to ship them out tomorrow.
There must have like 50, 60 people there.
And I remember going-- there were rumors that they're going to ship them tomorrow.
So it was already lights out.
I'm not supposed to leave my barrack.
But I went to the barrack.
And there's an SS man standing there.
And I said, can I-- he said, where are you going?
Aren't you supposed to be in your barrack?
I said, yeah.
But I want to see my dad.
He's here.
Your dad is here?
He said, well, don't worry.
He'll be OK.
Get out.
Get out.
Get out of here.
It's already curfew.
And I said, please let me in.
I couldn't tell him that I heard rumors they're going to ship them, because he would say, where do know it from?
But it was true.
They were shipped the second day.
I said, I'd like to-- I understand they're going to go to a different detail, some other work.
I don't know how soon we see each other.
Can I say bye to him?
He says, get out of here.
And I said, please.
So he says, how old are you?
And I said, 17.
And he says, sure, you don't look it.
You're kind of little, skinny too.
I don't know why he asked me that.
But now I think maybe he had a boy, a son my age.
So he looked at this.
He says, [GERMAN].
And I said, [GERMAN] and stood at attention.
I run in.
And this is a-- this scene haunts me and probably will continue to haunt me the rest of my life.
I ran into the barrack-- there were like three, four different rooms in that barrack.
All one floor.
And I run in, and I start calling, Touchy, Touchy, Abraham!
And I hear someone, here, here, here-- a faint voice.
And I ran to that room.
And I saw my dad sitting on a bunk with a stroke.
And the commandant-- a very thin blanket, no pillow.
The blanket around him, shivering a little bit.
[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]
I wanted to-- I wanted to embrace him.
And he kind of bent down, so I put my hands around.
In a standing position, I could embrace.
But [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] it's kind of-- he was stooped down is the best term I can think of.
He was stooped down with his blanket over.
And I said, Dad, it's going to be OK, maybe?
He says, yeah.
Probably.
They say they're going to send us to hospital.
I got some sores that don't heal too well.
He says, I'm sure they'll give me something for that.
He said, don't worry.
I'll be OK.
I'll be OK.
And whatever else we said.
He says, you know, I wonder where Mom is.
Maybe-- she was a good cook.
She's probably-- she's probably in one of the work camps.
I bet she's cooking up a storm.
They're going to love her with her cooking.
You know, talk about Mom.
And then talk about Max and where he is.
And then we start embracing again.
And then my five minutes were up.
And I said, I gotta go, Dad.
And he looked at me.
And he said something I wish he wouldn't say.
He said, would you have a piece of bread on you?
[CRYING] Sometimes I would carry bread in my pocket.
When they give the evening ration, which was larger than the noon ration, I would eat half and put half away.
And that evening, I must-- I must have had exceptionally big hunger.
I ate the whole thing.
I didn't have any bread on me.
I couldn't give him.
